text: What is a class and what is an object — can you give a simple real-world example of each?
What is inheritance in OOP — can you give a simple real-world analogy to explain it?
What is a design pattern — why do developers use patterns?

What is a JOIN in SQL — what does it do?
What is a primary key in a database table?

What is cloud computing — why would a company use it?
What does an operating system do — why do we need one?
What is a network protocol — why do computers need to agree on rules?
What is machine learning — can you describe in plain terms how a machine can learn from data?
What is an average — can you calculate the average of five numbers and explain what it tells you?
What is a decision tree — can you describe how it makes a decision using a simple example?
What is data storage — why does a large company need to store and organise enormous amounts of data?
What is a frontend in a web application — what does it do?
What is a backend framework — why would a developer use one?
What is Docker — what problem does containerisation solve?
What is a cache — can you give a simple real-world example?
What is manual testing — what does a tester do when they test a web application by hand?
What does a library management system do — what are the basic things a librarian needs to track?
What is a bank account — what are the basic operations you can perform on one?
What is a notification in software — can you name three different ways an app might notify you?
What are the four pillars of OOP — can you name them and give a one-line description of each?
What is the difference between single and multiple inheritance?
What is the Singleton pattern and when would you use it?

What is the difference between INNER JOIN and LEFT JOIN?

What is Redis and when would you use it instead of querying the database directly?
What is Selenium — how does it automate browser-based testing?

If you were building a notification system that sends email and SMS, how would you structure it so the two channels do not depend on each other?

What is the difference between an abstract class and an interface, and when would you use one over the other?
What is the Factory design pattern and how does it help when you need to create objects without specifying the exact class?
What is database normalisation and can you walk me through 1NF, 2NF, and 3NF?
What is a window function in SQL and when would you use ROW_NUMBER or RANK?
How do primary keys and foreign keys work together to maintain referential integrity?

What is the difference between unit testing, integration testing, and end-to-end testing?
What is Agile and what does a typical Scrum sprint look like — what happens in each ceremony?
What is the difference between AWS, Azure, and IBM Cloud at a high level — how do you decide which to use?
What is virtual memory and how does the OS use paging to manage it?
What is the OSI model — can you name the seven layers and explain what each one is responsible for?
What is the difference between supervised and unsupervised machine learning, and can you give an example of each?
What is A/B testing, and can you walk me through how you would set one up to test whether a new feature improves user engagement?
What is the difference between overfitting and underfitting in a machine learning model, and how do you fix each?
What is the difference between a data warehouse and a data lake, and when would a company use each?
What is the difference between props and state in React, and how do they affect how a component renders?
How would you design a REST API using Node.js or Django — what are the key steps and decisions?
What is Docker and what problem does containerisation solve for a development team?
What is Redis and how would you use it to cache the results of a slow database query — when would you NOT use it?
What is the difference between functional testing and performance testing, and what tools are commonly used for each?
How would you design a library management system — what are the entities, how do you handle book availability, and how do you model borrowing and returns?
How would you design a banking system from an OOP perspective — what are the core classes, and how do you model different account types and transaction rules?
How would you design a notification service that sends alerts by email, SMS, and push — and how would you build it so adding a new channel later does not break anything?
What are the four pillars of OOP — can you explain each one with a real-world example?
What is memory in a program — why does a variable need memory to exist?
What is a variable in programming — what does it mean to store a value in memory?
What is a CPU and what does it do — why does it need to access data quickly?
What is an embedded system — can you give two examples of everyday devices that have one?
What is database normalisation and why do we do it?
What is a lock in a database — why would a database lock a row during a transaction?
What is an API — can you describe in plain terms what it does and why applications use them?
What is caching — can you give a simple real-world example?
What is the difference between RAM and a hard drive — why does your computer have both?
What is a program — what is the difference between a program sitting on disk and one that is running?
What happens when you tap your card at a shop — basic steps from tap to approval?
What happens when you order food on an app like Zomato — basic steps from order to delivery?
What does a search feature do — what happens when you type a restaurant name into a food delivery app?
What is a CRM system — what kind of business uses one and what problem does it solve?
What is a support ticket — what information does it typically contain and who creates it?
What is a microservice — how is it different from a regular application?
What is a digital wallet — how is it different from a regular bank account?
What happens when you book a movie ticket online — steps from choosing a film to confirmation?
What is a REST API — can you describe what happens when a mobile app makes an API call to a server?
What is a report in a business application — what kind of questions does it help a manager answer?
What is payment fraud — can you give a simple example?
What does it mean for a software system to handle a lot of users?
What is the difference between a payment succeeding and a payment failing?
What is high availability in software — why would an ecommerce site going down for an hour be a serious problem?
What does multi-tenant mean in software?
What is a cache — why would storing the result of a database query in memory make subsequent requests faster?
What is real-time tracking — can you describe how a food delivery app shows you where your driver is?
What is the difference between an abstract class and an interface — when would you use each?
What is the difference between stack and heap memory in C++ — when does a program use each?
What is a pointer in C++ — how is it different from a regular variable?
What is a CPU cache and why is it faster than RAM — what is the difference between L1, L2, and L3 cache?
What is an interrupt in an embedded system — why would a processor stop what it is doing to handle one?
What is a database index and how does it make a query faster?
What is deadlock in a database — can you describe a simple scenario?
What is the difference between REST and GraphQL — how does each one decide what data to return to the client?
What is Redis and when would you use it instead of querying the database directly?
What is virtual memory — why does the OS give each process its own virtual address space?
What is the difference between a process and a thread — do they share memory?
If you were designing a simple payment processing system, what are the core components and how would you handle a payment that fails?
If you were modelling a food delivery system in code, what would be the main entities?
If you were building a restaurant search system that filters by cuisine, rating, and distance, how would you model those filters?
If you were modelling a CRM in code, how would you represent the relationship between an Account and its Contacts?
If you were modelling a ticketing system, what states would a ticket go through from creation to resolution?
If you have ten microservices and a client needs to talk to all of them, what problems does that create?
If you were modelling a wallet system that supports both a regular balance and a fixed deposit, how would you represent those two account types in code?
If two users try to book the last available seat at exactly the same time, what could go wrong?
If you were designing a REST API for a payment service, what endpoints would you need and what HTTP status codes would you return?
If you had to build a reporting system that pulls data from thousands of accounts and generates a summary, how would you keep it fast?
If you were building a fraud detection system, what signals or patterns would you look for?
If your food delivery platform suddenly gets ten times the orders during a lunch rush, what parts of the system would struggle first?
If a payment request is sent to your server but the server crashes before sending a response, how do you make sure the user is not charged twice?
If your ecommerce platform is expecting a flash sale with ten times normal traffic, what changes would you make in advance?
If you are building a cloud CRM that serves thousands of businesses, how do you make sure one company's data is completely invisible to another?
If you were adding a caching layer to a high-traffic API, how would you decide what to cache and for how long?
If you were building a real-time order tracking system, how would the delivery partner's location get from their phone to the customer's screen?
What is OOP and can you explain the four main principles with a real-world example for each — and how did you apply them in your final year project?
How does memory management work in C++ — what is the difference between stack and heap, and when would a memory leak occur?
Can you explain what a pointer is in C++, how pointer arithmetic works, and what a dangling pointer is?
What is the difference between a RISC and CISC processor architecture, and which approach do modern processors lean towards?
What is the difference between I2C and SPI as communication protocols, and when would you choose one over the other?
What is the difference between an ACID transaction and eventual consistency — and when would a fintech company like PayPal choose one over the other?
What is deadlock in the context of databases, and how do systems like Oracle detect and resolve it?
What is the difference between REST and GraphQL, and in what situation would you recommend GraphQL over REST?
What is Redis and how does caching with Redis work — when would you use it versus querying the database directly?
What is virtual memory and how does the OS use paging to manage it — can you explain what a page fault is?
What is the difference between a process, a thread, and a coroutine — and which would you use when building a high-concurrency payment service?
How would you design a payment processing system — what are the core components, how do you handle a failed transaction, and how do you make sure a payment is never processed twice?
How would you design a food delivery system like Zomato — what are your core entities, how do you model the relationship between restaurants, orders, and delivery partners?
How would you design a restaurant search system — how do you model filters like cuisine, rating, and distance, and how do you keep results fast?
How would you design a CRM system — how do you model Accounts, Contacts, and Opportunities, and how do you enforce business rules?
How would you design a ticketing and incident management system like ServiceNow — what are the core entities and how does a ticket move through its lifecycle?
How would you design an API gateway for a microservices-based ecommerce platform — what responsibilities does it have and how does it handle authentication and rate limiting?
How would you design a wallet system that supports fixed deposits and cashback rewards — what are your core classes and how do you model the different account types?
How would you design a BookMyShow-style event booking system — how do you model events, seats, and bookings, and how do you prevent two users from booking the same seat?
How would you design a REST API for a financial transactions service — what endpoints do you expose, how do you handle idempotency, and what status codes do you return?
How would you design a reporting and analytics engine that aggregates data across millions of records and generates summaries on demand?
How would you design a fraud detection model for a payment platform — what signals would you use and how would you structure the system to flag suspicious transactions in real time?
How would you design the backend for a food delivery platform like Zomato at scale — how do you handle peak ordering times and keep delivery assignments fast?
How would you design a payment processing system at the scale of PayPal — how do you ensure no payment is processed twice and how do you handle failures mid-transaction?
How would you design a high-availability ecommerce platform like Flipkart for a sale event with ten times normal traffic — where are the bottlenecks?
How would you design a cloud-based CRM platform like Salesforce that serves millions of businesses — how do you isolate data between tenants and keep the platform performant?
How would you design a caching layer for a high-traffic API — how do you decide what to cache, for how long, and how do you handle stale data?
How would you design a real-time order tracking system — how does the location of a delivery partner get updated and pushed to the customer?

What is the difference between RAM and storage — why does your computer have both?
What does it mean for data to be consistent — why does it matter that two users see the same information?
What does it mean when we say a system needs to scale?
What is Salesforce and what kind of problems does it help businesses solve?
What is a deployment in software development — what does it mean to push code to production?
What is a code review and why do development teams do them?

What is a URL shortener — what problem does it solve and can you name one you have used?
What is a cache and why do applications use one?
What happens when you place an order on an ecommerce site — steps from clicking buy to receiving the package?
What is a notification in software — can you name three different ways an app might notify you?
What is a document management system — can you name one you have used and describe what it does?
What does the undo feature in a text editor do — what happens when you press Ctrl+Z?
What is OOP — can you describe what a class and an object are using a real-world example?
What is a CRM system — what kind of data does a business store in one?
What is a collaborative tool — can you describe how something like Google Docs allows multiple people to work simultaneously?
What happens when you book a ride on an app like Uber — steps from opening the app to arriving at your destination?
What is a key-value store — can you give a simple real-world example?
What is a backend in a web application — what does it do?
What is a scheduled job in software — can you give an example of a task that a system might run automatically at a fixed time every day?
What is the difference between a regular HTTP request and a WebSocket connection?
What is a stock exchange at a basic level — what happens when someone wants to buy a share and someone else wants to sell one?
What is financial risk in simple terms — why would a bank or trading firm want to monitor it continuously?
What is payment fraud — can you give an example of how someone might fraudulently use another person's card?
What is a log file — why do large applications generate them?
What happens when you click a shortened URL — steps between clicking the link and arriving at the destination?
What is the difference between storing data in memory versus storing it on disk?
What is state in a frontend application — why does a React component need to keep track of state?
If two threads both try to update the same variable at the same time, what can go wrong and why?
What happens when your computer runs out of RAM — does the program crash immediately or does something else happen first?
If your computer is running ten programs at the same time but only has one CPU, how does it manage that?
What happens when two threads on different CPU cores keep overwriting each other's cached data — why does that slow things down?
What is the difference between a spinlock and a mutex — how does each one wait for a lock to become available?
If two threads are both waiting for each other to release a lock, what happens — can they ever move forward?
What is virtual memory — why does the OS give each program the illusion that it has its own private memory space?
If you update your profile picture on a social network and your friend still sees the old one for a few seconds, is that a problem?
What is the difference between adding more power to one server versus adding more servers?
What is Apex and how is it different from a general-purpose language like Java?
If you notice your application is suddenly running much slower after a new release, what are the first two or three things you would check?
What practices would you put in place to make sure code quality does not drop when your team members are working at different hours?
If you were modelling a parking lot in code, what would be the main objects you would need?
If you had to build a simple URL shortener, what would the basic flow look like?
What does LRU mean — if your cache is full and a new item comes in, which item do you remove and why?
If you were modelling an order in code, what states would it go through?
If you were building a notification system that sends both email and SMS, how would you structure the code so that the two channels do not depend on each other?
If you were building a simple document management system, what are the core operations it needs to support?
If you were building a text editor that supports undo, what data structure would you use to keep track of changes?
If you were modelling a chess game in code, what would be the main objects you would need?
If you were modelling a CRM in code, what would be the core objects like Accounts and Contacts?
If two people are drawing on a shared whiteboard at the same time and both make changes to the same area, how would you decide which change wins?
If you were modelling a ride-sharing app in code, what would be the main objects?
If you were building an in-memory key-value store that automatically deletes entries after a set time, how would you track when each entry was added?
If your task management app suddenly had ten times more users, what parts of your backend would struggle first?
If you had a job scheduler sending tasks to ten remote workers and one went offline mid-task, how would you make sure the task still gets completed?
If you were building a notification system that pushes alerts to users in real time, how would you make sure a message is delivered even if the user's connection drops briefly?
If you were building a simple order book for a stock exchange, how would you match a buy order with a sell order?
If you were building a system that watches a trader's portfolio and sends an alert when their exposure crosses a threshold, what are the core components?
If you were building a system to detect fraudulent transactions, what signals or patterns would you look for?
If you had a hundred microservices each writing their own log files, what problems would that create?
If your URL shortener is handling a million clicks a day, what parts of the system would you be most worried about?
If your key-value store needs to handle millions of reads and writes per second, what would be the first bottleneck you would hit?
If two users are editing the same document at the same time in a browser, how do you make sure both see each other's changes without refreshing the page?
Two threads are both trying to increment a shared counter at the same time — why does the final count end up being less than expected, and how would you fix that?
What actually happens at the operating system level when a program tries to use more memory than the machine physically has?
Can you explain what a context switch is — what gets saved, what gets restored, and why the OS needs to do it at all?
What is cache thrashing, and can you walk me through a real scenario where it would slow down a multi-threaded application?
What is the difference between a spinlock and a mutex, and in a latency-sensitive system like high-frequency trading, which would you reach for and why?
If two threads try to grab the same lock at the same time, what happens? And can you describe a classic scenario where two threads end up in a deadlock?
Why does an operating system use virtual memory, and can you explain in plain terms how the OS translates a virtual address into a physical one?
What is the difference between strong consistency and eventual consistency, and can you give me a real-world example of a situation where eventual consistency is actually acceptable?
What is the difference between scaling up and scaling out, and if your SaaS product suddenly gets ten times the traffic, which approach would you go with and why?
How is Apex different from Java, and what are the governor limits in Salesforce that a developer absolutely must keep in mind when writing triggers?
You push a deployment and within five minutes your latency triples. Talk me through your thought process — what do you check first and how do you decide whether to roll back?
How do you make sure code quality stays high when your team is working asynchronously across different time zones?
If I asked you to design a parking lot system from scratch — one that handles different vehicle sizes and multiple floors — how would you think through the classes and relationships involved?
How would you design a URL shortening service like bit.ly — what are the key components, how do you generate the short codes, and how do you handle two users getting the same code?
How would you design an LRU cache — what data structures would you combine to make both reads and writes happen in constant time?
Walk me through how you would design an order management system — what are the core objects, and how does an order move through its different states from placement to return?
How would you design a notification service that needs to send alerts by email, SMS, and push notification — and how would you build it so adding a new channel later does not break anything?
Walk me through how you would design a document management system like Google Drive — focusing on the core operations and how you would handle permissions for different users.
How would you design a text editor that supports undo and redo — what data structure powers that feature and how does it work?
If you had to design a chess game using OOP principles, how would you model the board, the pieces, and the rules — including how you detect check?
Walk me through how you would model a CRM system like Salesforce — what are the core objects, how do they relate, and how do you enforce business rules?
How would you design a collaborative whiteboard where multiple people can draw at the same time — how do you handle two people editing the same area simultaneously?
If you were designing a ride-sharing app like Uber from an OOP perspective, how would you model the core entities and how does a ride move through its different states?
How would you design an in-memory key-value store that supports get, put, and delete — and how would you extend it to automatically expire keys after a set amount of time?
How would you design the backend for a task management app that needs to handle ten times its current traffic — what does your architecture look like and where are the bottlenecks?
How would you design a distributed job scheduler that sends tasks to remote workers — and what happens when a worker goes down mid-task?
How would you design a real-time notification system for a remote team tool — how do you make sure messages are delivered reliably over WebSockets even at high scale?
How would you design a trade matching engine — how does the order book work, how do you match buyers and sellers, and how do you handle multiple orders coming in at the same time?
How would you design a real-time risk monitoring system for a trader's portfolio — what does the architecture look like and how do you keep the alert latency as low as possible?
How would you design a fraud detection system for live payment transactions — how do you spot suspicious behaviour without accidentally blocking a legitimate payment?
How would you design a centralised logging system that collects logs from thousands of microservices and lets engineers search through them in near real-time?
How would you design a URL shortener that needs to handle millions of redirects every second — what does the architecture look like and how do you make sure it stays available?
How would you design an in-memory key-value store that needs to support millions of reads and writes per second, with automatic key expiry built in?
If you were building the frontend for a real-time collaborative document editor, how would you manage shared state across users and keep everyone's view in sync?
Did you enjoy your Computer Science degree?
What do you like to do when you are not studying?
What is one thing you are good at?
Can you tell me a little about yourself — beyond just your name and college?
Tell me about your college life — what you studied and what you took away from those years.
Why did you choose Computer Science — was it always the plan or did something lead you there?
How would someone who knows you well describe you as a person?
What kind of work are you drawn to — and what does that tell you about yourself?
Walk me through what shaped you — not just your resume, but you as a person.
Looking back at your degree — what challenged you most, and did it change how you think?
What drew you to Computer Science — and now that you have lived it, does that reason still hold?
If you had to be honest — what is one thing you are genuinely good at, and one thing you are still figuring out?
How would the people closest to you describe who you are — and how much do you agree with that picture?
Your manager gives you a task but you do not understand what to do. What do you do?
You finish your work early and your teammate looks stuck. What do you do?
You push wrong code to the shared folder and your team lead sees it. What do you do?
One person in your project group is not doing their part. The deadline is tomorrow. What do you do?
You have to test a feature but you cannot access the testing system. What do you do?
Has there been a time you missed a deadline? What happened?
Tell me about a time someone in your group was not pulling their weight.
Has there been a time you had to follow instructions you did not like? What did you do?
Tell me about a time you had to do something you had never done before.
Has someone ever criticised your work? What happened?
You spot a bug your manager already approved — do you raise it or let it go? What do you do?
Two teammates disagree about how to split the work and both turn to you. How do you handle it?
Your manager asks you to work the weekend but you have plans. What do you do — and how do you say it?
You see a colleague breaking a rule that nobody else has noticed. Do you say something? What do you do?
Tell me about a time you had too many things at once — how did you decide what mattered most?
Tell me about a time a project went wrong — what broke down and what did you do about it?
Has there been a time you disagreed with a technical choice your team made? What did you do?
Tell me about a time you were under real pressure to finish something — what did you sacrifice and what did you protect?
Has there been a time you needed help but found it hard to ask? What happened?
Tell me about a time you stepped up in a group without being asked to. What moved you to do it?
Your code works but you know it is messy. You are out of time. Do you ship it — and how do you justify that decision?
A new colleague is clearly lost and nobody is helping them. What does that situation ask of you?
Your instructions are unclear and your deadline is close. Where is the line between asking for help and figuring it out yourself?
You find an error an hour before the deadline. Fixing it properly takes two hours. What do you do — and what does that choice say about you?
Someone takes credit for work that was partly yours. What do you do — and what stops most people from doing what you would do?
Tell me about a time you had to change course completely midway through something. What did letting go of the original plan feel like?
Walk me through how you debug a problem — not just the steps, but how you think.
Has there been a time you had to learn something fast because you had no choice? What did that reveal about how you learn?
Tell me about a group project where things nearly fell apart. What held it together — and what was your role in that?
Tell me about a time you helped someone understand something difficult. What did that teach you about how you explain things?
Are you okay to relocate to another city for this job?
Are you applying to other companies right now?
What salary are you expecting?
Do you have any questions for us?
Where do you see yourself in three years — and does this role fit into that picture?
Why this company — what is it about them specifically that interests you?
When can you join — and is there anything that might change that?
What do you know about what this company actually does?
Is there something you wanted to say in this interview that you did not get a chance to?
If we offered you this role today — what would make you say yes, and what would give you pause?
What would your first thirty days here look like — and what would you need from us to make them count?
If you do not get this role — what does that tell you, and what do you do next?
What is one thing you hope we remember about you after this conversation ends?
On a scale of one to ten — how ready do you think you are for this role, and what sits behind that number?
What is one technical thing you genuinely enjoy working on?
Did you enjoy your degree — what was your favourite part of it?
Can you tell me about yourself — and what you have been working on that you are most interested in?
Walk me through your academic background and the kind of work you have done alongside it.
Why Computer Science — and has your reason for choosing it shifted as you have studied more?
How would a teammate who has worked closely with you describe your working style?
What kind of engineer do you want to be in five years — and what are you doing now to move towards that?
Walk me through your resume — and tell me what you are most proud of and why it matters to you.
Tell me about your academic journey — what genuinely challenged you, and how did that shape the engineer you are becoming?
Why Computer Science — and looking back now, what would you have done differently to prepare yourself better?
What do you think makes you a genuinely different kind of candidate — not just stronger, but different?
How would you describe the gap between what your degree gave you and what the industry actually needs from engineers?
You are mid-way through building a feature and realise your approach will break for some cases. Reworking it delays the sprint. What do you do?
Your code passes unit tests but fails in integration testing the day before release. Your manager says ship it. What do you do?
Two senior developers disagree on which database to use. Both ask for your opinion. What do you do?
You are given a task outside your skillset with a three-day deadline. What do you do?
A critical bug is reported in production. You are the only developer available but it is not your module. What do you do?
Tell me about a time you had a tight deadline. How did you manage it and what did you give up?
Has there been a time you disagreed with your team on a technical decision? What happened?
Tell me about a time a miscommunication caused something to go wrong in a project. What did you do?
Has there been a time you had to learn a new tool or framework mid-project? How did you handle it?
Tell me about a time you had to juggle multiple technical tasks. How did you prioritise?
Your manager asks you to implement something you think is technically the wrong call. Do you push back — and how?
You join a project mid-sprint and nothing is documented. Your first task is due in two days. Where do you even start?
Your teammate is cutting corners on testing to hit the deadline. Real users will be affected. What do you do — and what makes it complicated?
A requirement was misunderstood from the start and your sprint ends tomorrow. How do you handle the damage?
Two managers both say their task is the priority. You cannot do both. How do you navigate that?
Tell me about a time you spotted a bug or gap that everyone else had missed. What did you do with that?
Tell me about a time you had to explain a complex technical problem to someone non-technical. What did that require of you?
Has there been a time you pushed back on something a senior asked you to do? What happened — and would you do it the same way again?
Tell me about a time a requirement changed halfway through. How did you adapt — and what did that cost you?
Tell me about a time a technical decision you made turned out to be wrong. What did you do — and what did it teach you?
A client escalates a complaint about something you built directly to your manager. What does that situation demand from you — technically and personally?
You are reviewing a junior developer's code and it is seriously flawed. How do you give feedback that is honest without being damaging?
Your team makes a technical decision in a meeting and nobody asks for your view. Do you speak up — and if so, how do you do it without derailing things?
You are about to demo a feature to a client that has a known bug. It may not show up. What do you do — and what is the right thing to do here?
Your manager is unreachable and a production issue just came in. There is no escalation path. What does this situation require of you?
Have you ever noticed something in a project that felt ethically off — even if it was not officially a problem? What did you do with that feeling?
Tell me about a time you had to optimise something that was already working. What guided your decisions about what to change and what to leave alone?
Tell me about a time you took ownership of something that was not officially yours to fix. What made you step in?
Describe a time you were under real pressure and had to protect quality anyway. Where did you draw the line — and how did you hold it?
Has there been a time credit was distributed unfairly in a team? What did that reveal about how you handle recognition and fairness?
Are you open to relocating for this role?
What is your expected CTC and when can you start?
Are you currently interviewing elsewhere?
What do you know about what this company builds?
Do you have any questions for us?
Where do you see yourself in three to five years — and how does this role specifically fit into that path?
What excites you about working at a product company — and what do you think will be hardest about it?
What would your first month here look like — and what would you need to make it useful?
Is there something from this interview you feel you could have answered better?
What is the one thing you want us to remember about you after this conversation?
Looking at the role we described — where do you think you are strongest, and where is your real gap?
If we give you this offer and another company does too — how will you decide, and what does that decision say about what you value?
What is the hardest thing you think you will face in the first six months here — and how are you going to handle it?
What does your ideal manager look like — and what does that tell you about how you work best?
Why should we hire you over the other candidates we have seen today — and what would you say to someone who disagreed?
What kind of engineering work do you enjoy most?
What is one project you are genuinely proud of?
Tell me one thing about yourself that is not on your resume.
Can you walk me through who you are as an engineer — and what you have been most invested in recently?
Tell me about your background — your college, your projects, and what brought you to this point.
Why software engineering — and what keeps you genuinely motivated in it now?
How would a senior engineer who has worked alongside you describe your technical strengths?
What is the most technically complex thing you have built — and what specifically made it complex?
Walk me through what has genuinely shaped you as an engineer — not the CV line items, but the experiences that changed how you think.
Tell me about your journey as an engineer — what you have built, what has tested you, and where you are trying to get to.
Why software engineering — and now that you are here, what do you think the field actually demands that most people underestimate?
What is the most significant technical decision you have made — and knowing what you know now, what would you do differently?
How would you describe the engineer you are right now versus the one you are trying to become — and what stands between those two versions of you?
You find a security vulnerability in your API two days before launch. Your manager wants to ship. What do you do?
You are reviewing a colleague's pull request. It works but it will create real technical debt. How do you handle the review?
Three weeks into a sprint you realise the architecture will not scale to what you need. A rebuild takes a month. What do you do?
You are asked to build a feature that collects more user data than seems necessary. You have concerns. What do you do?
Your manager asks for a timeline you know is not achievable. They are under pressure from above. What do you do?
Tell me about a time you failed at something significant technically. What did you actually learn — not just what you tell interviewers?
Tell me about a time you had to move a team or stakeholder without having authority over them. How did you do it?
Describe a time doing the right thing technically was not the popular choice. What did you do?
Tell me about a time you had to rebuild something from scratch because the original was fundamentally broken. How did you have that conversation with your team?
Describe a time you worked on a project where the requirements kept shifting. How did you manage the uncertainty?
You are leading a small team and two senior engineers cannot agree on the system design. The timeline is at risk. How do you move forward — and what does leadership look like in that moment?
A junior teammate keeps writing low-quality code. Your tech lead asks you to handle it without making it formal. How do you approach it — and where is the line between coaching and escalating?
You discover your company is using an open source library without the right licence. Nobody else knows. What do you do — and what makes this harder than it looks?
A colleague takes credit for your solution in front of senior leadership. How do you handle it in the room — and what do you do afterwards?
Your team is building a feature that meets the business requirement but you believe it will harm the user experience. Do you raise it — and how far do you push?
Tell me about a time you had to give difficult feedback to a senior colleague about their work. What did you have to weigh before you said anything?
Describe a time you had to balance technical rigour against business pressure. Where did you draw the line — and are you at peace with where you drew it?
Tell me about a time you spotted a security or privacy risk others had missed. What made it hard to raise — and what did you do anyway?
Describe a time you had to rapidly get up to speed in an area outside your comfort zone to deliver something critical. What did that cost you?
Tell me about a time a team you were part of was coming apart under pressure. What held it together — and what was your actual role in that?
You are onboarding a new engineer and realise your team's documentation is dangerously outdated. Your sprint is already full. What does this situation ask of you — and what do you let slide?
Your manager asks you to sign off on a deployment you are not confident about. The business is pushing hard. Where is the line between deference and responsibility?
You discover that a performance optimisation your team is proud of introduces a subtle race condition under load. The fix is complex. How do you handle the technical problem — and the people problem?
A senior engineer consistently dismisses ideas from junior members in meetings. Morale is suffering. You are not their manager. What do you do — and what is the cost of doing nothing?
You are asked to cut testing coverage to hit a launch deadline for a product going to hundreds of thousands of users. What do you do — and how do you make the case for what you believe is right?
Describe a time you made an unpopular technical decision and stood your ground. What gave you the confidence to hold the line — and at what point would you have backed down?
Tell me about a time you discovered something your team had been doing for a long time was fundamentally wrong. How do you change a system that people are invested in?
Describe a time you had to ruthlessly prioritise — where you could not do everything and something had to be let go. How did you decide what mattered most, and what did you mourn?

You are working on an AI feature and realise it performs significantly worse for certain demographic groups. Your manager wants to ship on schedule. What do you do — and what is the cost of each choice available to you?
Describe a time you raised an ethical concern about a product or feature. How did you navigate that conversation — and what did you learn about how organisations handle these things?
Are you open to relocating for this role?
What is your expected compensation and when can you join?
Are you currently evaluating other offers?
What do you know about this company's engineering culture and what they are building?
Do you have any questions for us?
Where do you see yourself in five years — and how does this role specifically accelerate getting there?
What is it about this company specifically — beyond the brand and the salary — that makes you want to be here?
What kind of engineering problems do you most want to be working on two years from now — and why here?
Is there anything from this conversation you would like to come back to — something you feel you did not fully land?
What would make you turn down an offer from us — even if the package was right?
Given everything we have covered today — where do you think you genuinely fit this role, and where is the real gap you would have to close?
If you could redesign how you prepared for this interview — what would you do differently, and what does that tell you about how you learn?
What is the hardest engineering problem you think you will face here in year one — and what is your honest plan for handling it?
What does exceptional engineering work actually look like to you — and how far are you from that standard right now?
Why should we hire you — and two years from now, what does the version of you that made this team better actually look like?
